# Analysis and Conclusion

<!-- This is a comment. Below, you'll see code for inserting an image. To make this image appear, update <custom-path>. To add an image, save it inside the imgs folder of this repository. -->
<img src="FinalProject/static/imgs/logo.png" alt="Image of Comp110 rainbow logo. "  width="500"/>

## The goal of this analysis was to determine if implementing "Industry Application" workshops would create significant value for students in COMP110. Using survey data from the course, I focused my investigation on the non-Computer Science major population, as these students represent the primary stakeholders for interdisciplinary Python applications.
By utilizing a custom filtering function to isolate non-majors, I explored the relationship between a student's primary major and their desire to see computer science connected to other professional fields. The analysis specifically looked at three metrics: the total distribution of majors, the average interest score for interdisciplinary connections, and how that interest correlates with the overall perceived value of the course. This approach allowed me to verify if there is a legit demand for exposure to career-specific coding.

The data analysis performed on the survey_alyssa.csv supports the implementation of industry application workshops. By using the count function and our first bar chart, I identified that the majority of students in COMP110 are not Computer Science majors. More importantly, the second visualization showed that non-majors expressed a high level of interest in seeing the connections between computer science and other fields. Finally, the box plot illustrated that students who already find the course skills valuable are highly interested in these connections, suggesting that adding real-world applications would reinforce the course's overall value. 

I reccomend that the course introduces optional "Industry Track" modules. These would consist of ten minute supplementary videos or optional practice problems that demonstrate how concepts like while loops or dictionaries are applied in specific fields. This directly addresses the interests of the large non-CS population identified in the without slowing down the core curriculum. 

To refine the idea, the course could host guest speakers or showcase specific Python libraries used in other majors.
